# Prefer Automated Verification

**Flexibility: Expected (SHOULD)**

## The Principle

Engineering teams SHOULD automate verification — tests, linters, type
checks, security scans, contract validation — rather than relying on
human inspection as the primary quality gate. Automated checks SHOULD
run on every change, and their results SHOULD be required before merge.

## Concept Foundation

- **JOS-C22 (Feedback Loop Latency)** — Human inspection is a slow
  feedback loop. A reviewer may take hours or days to notice a defect
  that a machine can catch in seconds. Automated verification compresses
  feedback latency from days to minutes, allowing defects to be caught
  while the author still has full context.

## Why This Matters

Human attention is finite, expensive, and unreliable at repetitive tasks.
Asking a reviewer to manually verify that a function handles null inputs,
that a dependency has no known CVEs, or that a style guide is followed
wastes cognitive capacity that should be spent on design judgment and
architectural reasoning. Automation handles the repeatable checks; humans
handle the judgment calls. This division of labor improves both speed
and quality.

## Tension and Tradeoffs

Automated verification has diminishing returns at the margins. Some
properties — usability, architectural coherence, business logic
correctness — resist full automation and still require human judgment.
The principle does not claim automation replaces review; it claims
automation should handle everything it can so that human review focuses
on what only humans can evaluate. There is also a maintenance cost:
flaky tests, slow pipelines, and false-positive linters erode trust in
automation. Teams must invest in keeping their automated checks fast,
reliable, and meaningful.

## 1970/2070 Note

In 1970, verification meant manual desk-checking and scheduled test
runs. In 2070, AI agents may generate and maintain test suites
autonomously. The principle survives both eras because it speaks to
the division of labor between machine and human, not to any specific
tool or technique.

## Baseline+Overlay Note

This eng-domain principle extends JOS's universal governance. It operates
in addition to, not instead of, meta-layer principles.
